speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Walter Absil. When did you arrive in Canada and where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we, with the help of the HIAS, who paid part of my, for my trip, because we didn't have enough money to pay for our sea voyage, we embarked in the port of [NON-ENGLISH] on a liberty ship. Those liberty ships that were used to transport troops to Europe in the war were now used to transport immigrants to North America. And we arrived in the city of Quebec City. I believe it was September, 25, 1951. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We then were put on a train from Montreal, where the HIAS, again, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] directed us to a place where there would have been a room reserved for us. The only problem was that the room was occupied. We slept on the floor that first night in Canada. The next day we-- I did go to the HIAS and the Jewish Immigration Services, and they, again, gave us an address where we could rent a room. And after looking in my passport they saw that I was the proud owner of $200, which my uncle had lent me. And they then told me that I was wealthy and that that I didn't need any help.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to that little room and rented the room. And, and I looked for work. Again, I went to the Jewish vocational services. And they told me, come back after the holidays. It was just before Yom Kippur and Rosh Hashanah. And I found a job selling stationery. And my boss hired me in French. She was a Montreal Jew that spoke some French. And I never looked back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually, I went-- became a partner in that firm. And when we sold out to a conglomerate, I went into business for myself. And I just sold it last year.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your business, Mr. Absil?

speaker: interviewee
text: I imported tools. I had a brand name of tools from Japan that I distributed exclusively in North America.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the band?

speaker: interviewee
text: Olfe, O-L-F-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Canada with two children that were born in, in Belgium. And just about nine months after reaching Canada, our Canadian was born, Karin. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so we have three children. And today we have six grandchildren. And we have one great-grandchild on the way. So we really worked very hard in replacing all the people that are no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Absil, how do you feel about Canada? How do you think they helped you, formed your, your life, what, what kind of help you got?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as, as I just told you, I got very little help. I never got any money whatsoever from the Jewish Immigrant Society in Canada. I did get some help paying for my, my, my sea voyage.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm talking about Canada as a whole, as a country.

speaker: interviewee
text: Canada as a whole was very, very good to me. I, I had motivation, a lot of motivation that some of the youngsters today don't have. I can only compare this to the ships that-- what was it-- Cortez burned in, in, in Mexico, where he landed with 200 troops and in order for the troops not to have any way to back off, he burned his own ships. And we arrived in Canada, and we really had nowhere to go but up. We had, we, we had to succeed. And I think that motivated me. And we also had very little luxuries that we, we demanded. So I recall that at the beginning, we had to buy a stove. And, and, and I bought four legs and that was the bed. You know, you had those-- I don't know how you call them-- those metal frames with four legs that you screwed on, and that was the extent of our furniture. Erika bathed our children in the kitchen sink. We didn't have a, a tub. So our demands were minimal. And we could only go up from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Absil, we're almost at the end of your interview. And when I'm sitting here and I'm thinking back to your story, one thing comes to mind, one thing that we forgot to talk about, and that was your name change. How did that come about?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were immigrants in Belgium. And the Germans started to pick up the Jews, immigrant Jews first. The Queen Mother Elisabeth of Belgium protected the Belgian Jews. Also the local populations would have probably been aroused. That's what the Germans thought.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Germans devised this plan. Let's pick up all the immigrants first and leave the, the Belgian Jews alone. Eventually, when they run out of immigrants, they, of course, deported the Belgian Jews also. But in 1942, we didn't know that. The only thing we knew was that if we could obtain the Belgian nationality, we would be safe. How do you obtain that Belgian nationality? Well, for female, of course, the easiest way to obtain it is to marry a Belgian. We had two unmarried females, my sister, who was about 15, and my grandmother, who was 71. Well, who do you try to protect first? You try to protect the young girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: My dad picked up a [NON-ENGLISH], who was a driver for the Belgian Red Cross and who needed money for the bars every weekend. And he agreed to marry whoever as long as he gets paid. And this is exactly what we did. By the time we found out, which was three months hence, that my sister, being underage, not being 16 yet, needed a special permit from the German from Berlin, which of course, was out of the question, we had a paid-up groom on our hands. My grandmother married him. My grandmother, not looking at the man, without speaking one word of French, married Mr. [NON-ENGLISH], became a Belgian subject. Poor Mr. [NON-ENGLISH], a few months later while under the influence, fell down a flight of stairs and broke his neck. So my grandmother was a widow. We didn't have to go through the expense of a divorce.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your, your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, now, this was not our first try at obtaining that nationality. The second way to obtain a nationality in Belgium is as follows. If you lived during a certain period of your life in Belgium and-- an adoptive parent is in all regards the same as a real parent.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Mrs. Absil, who was-- Absil was her maiden name in Belgium. The women keep their maiden name all their life. She was a widow, and she agreed to adopt me. There, it wasn't for money. She really tried to help. And while my dad might have given her some gifts, she didn't want to accept any money, even though we had to drag her to city hall and back and to the lawyer's and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she adopted me. And my name was changed from the name of my parents, Bondy, to the name of Absil. I have a certificate to that effect.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Mr. Absil, I, I have some other questions. You've been through a lot during the Holocaust. You're hiding, and, and you lost your parents. Do you often think about these experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Especially lately. I mean, for 50 years, you kind of put those things out of your mind. And you didn't speak about them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever have-- do you ever have nightmares?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never considered myself a victim. I, I, I never approach anything-- when my children talk to you, they, they-- we never told them that we were victimized.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you suppose all this helped to form your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, number one, I believe that I had very-- I still have very strict business ethics and ethics in my life. And I have an, an excellent name in any trade that I had dealt with, being it as a supplier or as a, as a purchaser. I recall waking at night, being awakened by the screams in the street. If Jewish blood flows from our life, we feel much better. Somehow I figured that maybe I did something. And, and maybe some of the Jews didn't behave properly. And I have always had this feeling that I've got a good, be a good boy in order not to trigger this kind of reaction. And I would say that in my business life, I've got the excellent name, because my ethics were exemplary. And the next thing that affected me is that when I was put in charge of grown ups, and I had to support them, and then as a young man, I had a family to support, it certainly inspired me and, and gave me this drive that made me today a wealthy man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you involved in any community work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm a, I'm a director of the Montreal Holocaust Memorial Center. And I'm a volunteer guide. And I'm volunteer speaker. I speak to a lot of children. I speak in German, French, or English. I go to the, to schools in French Canada, because in French Canada, they haven't even seen a Jew. They don't know what a Jew is.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I have a very funny incident where this teacher called us up and said my-- there was a, an antique store in Sherbrooke, which is a fairly large town in the province of Quebec, that displayed the Nazi flag in the window. And he wanted to explain to his children what happened and so forth. And he said-- and we said, why don't you come and visit our museum? Which he did. And it was great. And I spoke to the kids as a survivor. And that was fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And two weeks later, he called us up and said the children have decided to put on a play about the Holocaust. Would you please do us the honor to come out to Sherbrooke and, and, and attend the first opening of our play. So Erika, my wife, said you never wanted to go to the-- when kids had a play in school. Now we got to schlep to Sherbrooke.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to Sherbrooke. And we were received like royalty. They gave us white roses because they incorporated into the play the White Rose, the resistance group. I don't know if you ever heard of that. And they said, Mr. Absil, you, we did you the honor-- you, you, there is you in the play. One of the boys represented that person that's called Mr. Absil in the play.

speaker: interviewee
text: And your seats are reserved in the first row of the basement of the church of Notre Dame du Saint Rosaire, the Holy Rosary Church. OK. And we go and sit down. And a little boy behind us said, you're not allowed to sit here. This is reserved for the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there's a kid that saw people that looked fairly normal, that spoke French. They couldn't be the Jews. We didn't have any horns. So what I'm trying to tell you is that in most parts of the province of Quebec, they don't know what a Jew is or what a Jew looks like. And if nothing else, when I go to high schools and when I go to different parts of the province to talk to the kids, at least they can say, I saw a Jew, and he's really not that bad. If that's nothing-- well, that's something, at least. Mr. Absil, would you like to say something to your children and grandchildren now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the message, really, that I would like to leave is, like I said, I have been instrumental in making sure that all of you have a decent life. I have helped wherever it is necessary. I have not always helped in equal parts all over the place, because it wasn't necessary. But whoever was in need and whoever needed my help, it was there. And as long as I'm around, this is what I will continue doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're all here. You're only here because I-- not by my intelligence but by pure miracle and luck-- I walked through the valley of the shadow-- how is that famous? Psalm number 26? And I came out alive. And I'm here for you. Thank you very much, Mr. Absil.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's been a real honor.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. We're in Brussels, 1941. The Germans occupied the country and tried to educate the Belgians that the Jews are really evil. And those posters were all over the city, describing the, the Jewish conspiracy over Europe. We sighed and found it so ridiculous that my dad posed us, Walter Bondy and Liesl Bondy, in front of this stupid poster. In the left corner-- in the right corner, you see the V for Victory that the Belgian resistance has already applied to this poster.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this exactly taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in 1941. That's before the Germans ordered the, the Jews to come and pick up their star. Same poster on the wall of the Montreal Holocaust Memorial Center. Same people, 50 years later. I don't-- I didn't change that much. I lost a few hair, but that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the names of the-- full names of the people on the left-hand side?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's Walter and Liesl Bondy. And now we're called Walter Absil and Sister Elizabeth of the Carmelite--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, wait. Now.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is on the occasion of the 50th wedding anniversary. We got married just after liberation in Belgium, and we never had a religious ceremony. So what we did is we now finally got married in synagogue. And you have myself, Walter Absil, my wife, Erika, my son, Michael, my daughter Ingrid, and my daughter Karin. In the summer of '95, we visited Amsterdam. And there you have myself and the statue of Anne Frank and-- around the corner from the Anne Frank Museum in Amsterdam. My granddaughter [? Sidney ?] Jacobson wrote this in school when she was about five. Grammatical errors included, it's a very cute little letter. Knowing from experience how harsh life can be, I try to convey that feeling to my fellow Canadians. I try to tell them not to separate one province from the other part of Canada. And that's why I wrote this little article, which was published in I guess it didn't help very much.
